The high priest Ananias orders him struck; Paul responds: 'God is going to strike you, you whitewashed wall!' (23:3). Perceiving the Pharisee-Sadducee divide, Paul cries: 'I am a Pharisee! It is with respect to the hope and the resurrection of the dead that I am on trial' (23:6). The council erupts. 'The Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, nor angel, nor spirit, but the Pharisees acknowledge them all' (23:8). Pharisaic scribes defend Paul: 'What if a spirit or an angel spoke to him?' (23:9). That night, 'the Lord stood by him (episte de auto ho kyrios) and said, "Take courage, for as you have testified to the facts about me in Jerusalem, so you must testify also in Rome"' (23:11). A conspiracy of more than forty Jews vows not to eat until they have killed Paul (23:12-14). Paul's nephew alerts the tribune (23:16-22). Paul is transferred under heavy guard to Caesarea and the governor Felix (23:23-35). The divine plan is in motion: Paul will reach Rome.

acts_mission.title: Felix, Festus, Agrippa, and the Voyage to Rome -- The Gospel Reaches the Capital
acts_mission.synopsis: Paul's trial and journey to Rome occupy the final five chapters of Acts. Before Felix, Paul declares: 'I worship the God of our fathers, believing everything laid down by the Law and written in the Prophets, having a hope in God... that there will be a resurrection of both the just and the unjust' (24:14-15). Felix, 'well informed about the Way' (24:22), keeps Paul imprisoned for two years, hoping for a bribe (24:26). Festus succeeds Felix and proposes a Jerusalem trial; Paul appeals to Caesar: 'I appeal to Caesar' (25:11). Before King Agrippa II, Paul delivers his most comprehensive defense (26:1-29), recounting his Damascus road encounter a third time with the fullest commission statement: 'I am sending you to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power (exousia) of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me' (26:17-18). Agrippa's verdict: 'This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar' (26:32). The sea voyage to Rome (chapter 27) is a masterpiece of ancient maritime narrative. A storm threatens destruction; Paul is assured by an angel: 'Do not be afraid, Paul; you must stand before Caesar. And behold, God has granted you all those who sail with you' (27:24). They are shipwrecked on Malta (28:1-10). Paul finally reaches Rome (28:14-16). He meets with Jewish leaders and quotes Isaiah 6:9-10 -- the hardening prophecy. Acts ends with Paul 'proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness and without hindrance' (28:31).

key_verse.text: 'I am sending you to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me.'

original_terms: exousia tou Satana (the power/authority of Satan -- 'from the power of Satan to God' (26:18); the nations are under Satan's jurisdiction; Paul's mission transfers them to God's kingdom)
he hodos (the Way -- 'concerning the Way (tes hodou)' (24:14, 22); the self-designation of early Christians; not a new religion but the fulfillment of Israel's path)
meta parrhesias akolytos (with all boldness without hindrance -- the final words of Acts (28:31); the gospel preached in Rome, the center of the world's power, with complete freedom)
basileia tou theou (the kingdom of God -- Paul preaches 'the kingdom of God' in Rome (28:31); the divine council's reign advancing into the capital of the human empire)
angelos theou (angel of God -- 'there stood before me this night an angel of the God to whom I belong' (27:23); a divine council messenger reassures Paul during the storm)
acts_mission.ane_backdrop: Paul's appeal to Caesar (25:11) invokes the right of 'provocatio' -- a Roman citizen's legal right to have their case heard by the emperor. This legal mechanism becomes the means by which the divine council ensures Paul reaches Rome. The sea voyage of chapter 27 is one of the finest pieces of ancient maritime narrative, with technical details of navigation, ship types, and weather patterns that have been confirmed by maritime historians. The route from Caesarea to Rome via Myra, Cnidus, Fair Havens (Crete), Malta, Syracuse, Rhegium, and Puteoli covers approximately 2,000 nautical miles. The 'Euroclydon' (euraquilo, 27:14) was a northeast wind notorious in the central Mediterranean. The shipwreck narrative includes 276 persons (27:37) -- a detail typical of ancient historical precision. Paul's arrival in Rome fulfills both the commission of Acts 1:8 ('to the end of the earth' -- Rome was the center of the known world) and the Lord's direct promise (23:11, 'you must testify also in Rome'). The theological geography is complete: the gospel has traveled from Jerusalem, the center of the Jewish world, to Rome, the center of the Gentile world.

second_temple.summary: 'Go and say to this people: Keep on hearing, but do not understand; keep on seeing, but do not perceive. Make the heart of this people dull, and their ears heavy, and blind their eyes.'
second_temple.relevance: Paul quotes this as his final word to the Roman Jews (28:26-27), interpreting Israel's rejection of the gospel as the fulfillment of Isaiah's hardening prophecy -- the same text Jesus used (Mark 4:12; Luke 8:10) and that Romans 11:7-10 develops.

second_temple.summary: Josephus describes King Agrippa I's speech urging the Jews not to rebel against Rome, noting Agrippa II's close relationship with Rome and his role as a client king.
second_temple.relevance: Provides historical context for Paul's appearance before Agrippa II (Acts 25:13 - 26:32). Agrippa is a Herodian king, nominally Jewish, politically beholden to Rome -- the perfect audience for Paul's case that the gospel fulfills Jewish hopes without threatening Roman order.

cross_refs.note: 'Make the heart of this people dull' -- quoted by Paul as his final word to the Roman Jews (28:26-27); the hardening prophecy that closes the book
'A light for the Gentiles, that my salvation may reach to the end of the earth' -- the mandate fulfilled as the gospel reaches Rome
The nations allotted to the sons of God -- the framework Paul summarizes in 26:18: the nations are under 'the power of Satan' and Paul's mission reclaims them
'To him was given dominion and glory and a kingdom, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him' -- fulfilled as the gospel reaches the nations through Paul's ministry
'I am eager to preach the gospel to you also who are in Rome... it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes' -- Paul's letter anticipates his arrival in Acts 28
'What has happened to me has really served to advance the gospel... throughout the whole imperial guard' -- Paul's imprisonment in Rome advances the kingdom

acts_mission.divine_council_note: The final chapters of Acts bring the divine council narrative to its climax. (1) Paul's speech before Agrippa (26:1-29) contains the fullest statement of his commission: 'I am sending you to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power (exousia) of Satan to God' (26:17-18). This is the Deuteronomy 32 reversal expressed in its clearest terms: the nations are under Satan's exousia -- the authority structure established when the nations were allotted to the sons of God at Babel. Paul's mission is to transfer them 'from the power of Satan to God.' Every conversion is a territorial transfer, every church planted is a beachhead of the divine kingdom in enemy territory. (2) The sea voyage angel (27:23-24) is a divine council messenger: 'There stood before me this night an angel of the God to whom I belong and whom I worship, and he said, "Do not be afraid, Paul; you must (dei) stand before Caesar."' The divine necessity (dei) is the council's decree: nothing can prevent Paul from reaching Rome. (3) The ending of Acts is theologically deliberate. Paul quotes Isaiah 6:9-10 to the Roman Jews (28:26-27), then declares: 'Therefore let it be known to you that this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles; they will listen' (28:28). This is the final programmatic statement of Babel reversal: the salvation intended for all nations has reached the capital of the world. Paul preaches 'the kingdom of God' (28:31) -- the divine council's reign -- 'with all boldness and without hindrance (akolytos).' The last word of Acts, akolytos ('unhindered'), is the book's final theological claim: nothing -- not Jewish opposition, not Roman power, not the territorial spirits -- can hinder the advance of God's kingdom. The Babel judgment is being reversed. The nations are coming home.

sections.heading: Before Felix, Festus, and Agrippa (24:1 - 26:32)
sections.body: Before Felix, Paul declares: 'According to the Way (kata ten hodon), which they call a sect, I worship the God of our fathers, believing everything laid down by the Law and written in the Prophets' (24:14). He affirms: 'a hope in God... that there will be a resurrection of both the just and the unjust' (24:15). Felix, 'well informed about the Way' (24:22), keeps Paul two years, hoping for a bribe (24:26). Festus proposes a Jerusalem trial; Paul appeals to Caesar (25:10-11). Festus to Agrippa: 'The accused... had certain points of dispute with him about their own religion and about a certain Jesus, who was dead, but whom Paul asserted to be alive' (25:19). Before Agrippa, Paul delivers his fullest defense: 'I stand here on trial because of my hope in the promise made by God to our fathers, to which our twelve tribes hope to attain' (26:6-7). The Damascus road: 'A light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining around me' (26:13). The commission: 'I am sending you to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God' (26:17-18). Agrippa: 'In a short time would you persuade me to be a Christian?' (26:28). Paul: 'I would to God that... all who hear me this day might become such as I am -- except for these chains' (26:29). Verdict: 'This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar' (26:32). But the appeal is providential: the council has decreed that Paul will testify in Rome.

sections.heading: The Voyage to Rome and the Gospel Unhindered (27:1 - 28:31)
sections.body: Paul sails for Rome under guard (27:1). The 'we-passage' resumes (27:1) -- Luke is present. They sail via Myra, Cnidus, and Fair Havens on Crete (27:5-8). Paul warns against continuing: 'I perceive that the voyage will be with injury and much loss' (27:10). They sail anyway and are caught by the Euroclydon (27:14). After fourteen days without sun or stars, 'all hope of our being saved was at last abandoned' (27:20). Paul stands up: 'There stood before me this night an angel of the God to whom I belong... saying, "Do not be afraid, Paul; you must stand before Caesar. And behold, God has granted you all those who sail with you"' (27:23-24). They run aground on Malta; all 276 persons survive (27:44). On Malta, Paul is bitten by a viper and survives (28:3-6). He heals the father of Publius (28:8) and many others (28:9). After three months, they sail to Rome (28:11-14). Paul meets with Jewish leaders: 'It is because of the hope of Israel that I am wearing this chain' (28:20). Some believe, some disbelieve (28:24). Paul quotes Isaiah 6:9-10: 'The heart of this people has grown dull' (28:27). Then the programmatic conclusion: 'Let it be known to you that this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles (tois ethnesin); they will listen (autoi kai akousontai)' (28:28). 'He lived there two whole years... proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness and without hindrance (akolytos)' (28:30-31). Acts ends not with a period but with an open door: the gospel is loose in Rome, preached without hindrance. The Deuteronomy 32 reversal has reached the center of the world.

acts_pentecost.title: Pentecost: The Spirit Reverses Babel
acts_pentecost.synopsis: Pentecost (Shavuot) falls 50 days after Passover and celebrates both the wheat harvest and the giving of Torah at Sinai. God chose this day to pour out the Spirit because the new covenant (Jer 31:31-34) writes the law on hearts by the Spirit, not on stone tablets. The signs -- wind (pneuma, same word as 'spirit'), fire, and languages -- all echo Sinai: Exodus 19:16-19 describes wind, fire, and the sound of a trumpet. Jewish tradition (Midrash Tanchuma, Shemot 22) held that God's voice at Sinai split into 70 languages so all nations could hear. At Pentecost, the disciples speak in the languages of 'every nation under heaven' (2:5) -- a deliberate Babel reversal. At Babel (Gen 11), God confused languages to scatter rebellious humanity; at Pentecost, God gives languages to gather the nations back. Peter's sermon is a masterpiece of apostolic preaching: Joel 2:28-32 (the Spirit poured on 'all flesh'), Psalm 16:8-11 (the resurrection: David's body decayed but Jesus' did not), and Psalm 110:1 (the ascension: 'The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand'). The result: 3,000 baptized (2:41) -- a reversal of the 3,000 who died at the golden calf (Exod 32:28).

key_verse.text: Let all the house of Israel therefore know for certain that God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified.

original_terms.meaning: Weeks/Pentecost — the feast of covenant renewal; the day God chose to inaugurate the new covenant
Spirit/wind/breath — the ambiguity is intentional: God's breath-wind-Spirit fills the community
Tongue/language — both the physical organ and the spoken language; reversal of Babel's confusion
Lord — Peter applies YHWH's title to Jesus; 'God has made him both Lord (kyrios) and Christ'
Spirit/wind/breath (Hebrew) — the same word used in Gen 1:2 hovering over creation now creates a new humanity

acts_pentecost.ane_backdrop: Theophany at mountains was the standard divine communication model: Sinai with fire, cloud, thunder, and divine speech. The upper room Pentecost event transposes Sinai's mountain theophany into an urban setting. God is no longer tied to a sacred mountain or a temple; his presence fills an ordinary room and flows into the streets.
acts_pentecost.second_temple: Jewish tradition connected Shavuot to covenant renewal. At Qumran, the Community Rule (1QS 1:16-2:18) describes an annual covenant renewal ceremony at Pentecost. The Dead Sea Scrolls community saw themselves as the 'true Israel' of the new covenant. Luke presents the Spirit-filled church as the true fulfillment of what Qumran sought: a covenant community empowered by God's own Spirit.

cross_refs.note: Babel — languages confused to scatter; Pentecost reverses this by giving languages to gather
Sinai theophany — fire, sound, divine presence; Pentecost repeats these signs
Peter's primary text: 'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh' — now fulfilled
The new covenant written on hearts by the Spirit — inaugurated at Pentecost
3,000 died at the golden calf; 3,000 saved at Pentecost — grace reverses judgment

acts_pentecost.divine_council_note: Peter's sermon places Jesus at 'the right hand of God' (2:33) -- the seat of authority in the divine council (Psalm 110:1). From this position, Jesus 'has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing and hearing' (2:33). The Spirit's descent is the enthroned Son's first royal act: distributing divine power to his human representatives. The church becomes the embassy of heaven on earth.

sections.heading: Sinai, Babel, and the Birth of the Church
sections.body: Three Old Testament events converge at Pentecost. First, Sinai: God descends in fire and wind to constitute Israel as his covenant people. At Pentecost, fire and wind appear again to constitute the church as the new covenant people. Second, Babel: God confused languages as judgment on human autonomy. At Pentecost, God gives languages as a gift for mission. Third, the golden calf: 3,000 died under judgment (Exod 32:28). At Pentecost, 3,000 are saved by grace (Acts 2:41). The Spirit does not destroy but restores. The church is not a new institution but the fulfillment of Israel's entire story.

sections.heading: Peter's Sermon: Psalm 16, Psalm 110, and the Enthroned Messiah
sections.body: Peter builds his argument on two psalms. Psalm 16:10 ('You will not abandon my soul to Hades, or let your Holy One see corruption') cannot apply to David, who 'both died and was buried, and his tomb is with us to this day' (2:29). It must refer to David's greater descendant whose body did not decay -- Jesus, whom God raised. Psalm 110:1 ('The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand') means David's Lord has been enthroned. Peter's conclusion: 'God has made him both Lord (kyrios -- the LXX title for YHWH) and Christ (Messiah)' (2:36). This is not adoptionism -- Jesus is not becoming something new but being publicly installed in the role he has always held.

sections.heading: The First Community: Radical Sharing as Kingdom Economics
sections.body: The early church's practice is summarized in Acts 2:42-47: 'They devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers.' They 'had all things in common' (2:44) and 'sold their possessions and belongings and distributed the proceeds to all, as any had need' (2:45). This is not imposed communism but voluntary generosity flowing from the Spirit's work. They ate together 'with glad and generous hearts' (2:46). The result: 'the Lord added to their number day by day those who were being saved' (2:47). The community's life was its evangelism. People were drawn not by programs but by a visible alternative to Roman economic stratification.

acts_pentecost.title: Signs, Boldness, and the First Persecution
acts_pentecost.synopsis: The apostles' first miracle -- healing a lame man at the Beautiful Gate (3:1-10) -- provokes a public sermon and the first arrest. Peter and John are brought before the Sanhedrin, the same body that condemned Jesus weeks earlier. Peter, filled with the Spirit, preaches: 'There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved' (4:12). The council sees their 'boldness' (parrēsia) and recognizes them as uneducated men who 'had been with Jesus' (4:13). Ordered to stop preaching, they reply: 'We cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard' (4:20). The community prays for more boldness (4:29) and the place shakes (4:31). Ananias and Sapphira lie about their land sale and die -- not for keeping money but for lying to the Holy Spirit (5:3-4). 'Great fear came upon the whole church' (5:11). The apostles are arrested again; an angel releases them at night (5:19). Gamaliel advises caution: 'If this plan is from God, you will not be able to overthrow them' (5:39). The apostles are beaten and 'left the presence of the council, rejoicing that they were counted worthy to suffer dishonor for the name' (5:41).

key_verse.text: And there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.

original_terms.meaning: Boldness/free speech — a political term: the right to speak freely; the Spirit's gift to the persecuted
Name — in Semitic thought, the name carries the authority and character of the person
Name (Hebrew) — to act 'in the name' of Jesus is to act under his authority and mandate
Fellowship/sharing/communion — radical mutuality in the early community
Salvation/deliverance — comprehensive rescue: spiritual, physical, communal, cosmic

acts_pentecost.ane_backdrop: Healing at temple gates was a powerful public statement. The Beautiful Gate (probably the Nicanor Gate between the Court of Women and the inner courts) was a liminal space -- the lame man was excluded from full temple participation. His healing restores him to the worshipping community. Temple healing was understood as divine endorsement of the healer's authority.
acts_pentecost.second_temple: Gamaliel I (Gamaliel the Elder) was the grandson of Hillel and one of the most respected Pharisaic teachers. His counsel of patience (5:34-39) reflects the Pharisaic belief in divine providence: if something is from God, human opposition is futile. Josephus reports that Gamaliel's student Paul studied under him (cf. Acts 22:3). The Sanhedrin's concern was not theological debate but political control -- the apostles were claiming authority independent of the temple establishment.

cross_refs.note: The lame shall leap like a deer — messianic prophecy fulfilled in the Beautiful Gate healing
Peter quotes this again (4:11): the stone rejected by builders has become the cornerstone
Achan's sin of concealment — parallel to Ananias and Sapphira's deception
The early church prays Psalm 2 (4:25-26): 'Why do the nations rage?' — applying it to their situation
Shadrach, Meshach, Abednego refuse to bow — the same defiant obedience the apostles show

acts_pentecost.divine_council_note: Peter tells the Sanhedrin that Jesus is 'the stone that was rejected by you, the builders, which has become the cornerstone' (4:11). The 'builders' are Israel's leaders who function as God's delegated authority on earth. By rejecting the cornerstone, they have lost their authority. The apostles answer to a higher court: 'We must obey God rather than men' (5:29). When human authority contradicts divine authority, the heavenly council's decree prevails.

sections.heading: The Beautiful Gate: Restoration as Kingdom Sign
sections.body: The healing of the lame man (3:1-10) is not just a miracle but a kingdom sign. The man had been lame from birth (3:2) -- he had never walked. Peter says, 'I have no silver and gold, but what I do have I give to you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk!' (3:6). The man leaps and enters the temple 'walking and leaping and praising God' (3:8) -- fulfilling Isaiah 35:6. The kingdom of God restores what sin and curse have broken. The man was excluded from full temple participation due to his disability; now he enters freely. This is the gospel in microcosm: the excluded are welcomed, the broken are healed, and it all happens 'in the name' of the risen Jesus.

sections.heading: Parrēsia: The Spirit's Gift of Dangerous Speech
sections.body: The Greek word parrēsia ('boldness,' 'freedom of speech') appears repeatedly in Acts 4 (4:13, 4:29, 4:31). In the Greco-Roman world, parrēsia was a political right of citizens -- the ability to speak freely in the assembly. The Sanhedrin expects uneducated fishermen to cower. Instead they encounter men speaking with the confidence of the enthroned Christ. When the community prays for more boldness (4:29), the place shakes and they are filled with the Spirit (4:31). Boldness is not a personality trait but a Spirit-given capacity that emerges precisely in contexts of threat.

sections.heading: Ananias and Sapphira: Holiness in the New Community
sections.body: The Ananias and Sapphira episode (5:1-11) is Acts' most disturbing narrative. They sold property and secretly kept back part of the proceeds while claiming to give the full amount. Peter's indictment is precise: 'You have not lied to man but to God' (5:4). The lie was not about money -- Peter affirms their right to keep it all (5:4) -- but about pretending to be more generous than they were. In a community built on radical honesty and sharing, deception is an existential threat. The parallel to Achan (Josh 7) is deliberate: just as Israel's conquest was jeopardized by hidden sin, the church's mission cannot tolerate concealed hypocrisy. The judgment is severe because the stakes are cosmic.

acts_pentecost.title: Stephen: Israel's History as Divine Council Narrative
acts_pentecost.synopsis: The appointment of seven deacons (6:1-7) resolves a Hellenist-Hebrew tension over widow care. Stephen, 'full of grace and power' (6:8), performs signs and engages in theological debate with diaspora synagogues. False witnesses accuse him of speaking 'against this holy place and the law' (6:13). Stephen's defense (7:1-53) is the longest speech in Acts -- a sweeping retelling of Israel's history with a radical thesis: God has NEVER been confined to one place. Abraham was called in Mesopotamia (7:2), Joseph was God's agent in Egypt (7:9-16), Moses received revelation at the burning bush in Midian (7:30-34), and the tabernacle in the wilderness was God's preferred dwelling -- 'the Most High does not dwell in houses made by hands' (7:48, quoting Isa 66:1). Stephen's conclusion: 'You stiff-necked people... you always resist the Holy Spirit' (7:51). At the climax, 'full of the Holy Spirit, he gazed into heaven and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing (hestōta, not sitting) at the right hand of God' (7:55). Jesus STANDS -- the divine advocate rising to receive his first martyr. Stephen prays: 'Lord Jesus, receive my spirit' (7:59) and 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them' (7:60) -- echoing Jesus' own words on the cross. Among those watching: 'a young man named Saul' (7:58).

key_verse.text: But he, full of the Holy Spirit, gazed into heaven and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing at the right hand of God. And he said, 'Behold, I see the heavens opened, and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God.'

original_terms.meaning: Witness — from which 'martyr' derives; Stephen is the first to witness unto death
Stiff-necked — an OT covenant lawsuit term (Exod 33:3, Deut 9:6) accusing Israel of stubborn rebellion
Tent of testimony/tabernacle — God's mobile dwelling, which Stephen prefers to the fixed temple
Hard of neck (Hebrew) — stubborn resistance to God's leading, a covenant violation
Glory — the visible manifestation of God's presence that Stephen sees opened to him

acts_pentecost.ane_backdrop: The concept of the deity being 'uncontainable' by temples was known in the ancient world. Solomon himself acknowledged it at the temple dedication: 'Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you; how much less this house' (1 Kings 8:27). Stephen's argument was not anti-temple but anti-temple-idolatry -- the confusion of the building with the presence.
acts_pentecost.second_temple: Stephen's audience included members of the 'Synagogue of the Freedmen' (Libertinoi), diaspora Jews from Cyrene, Alexandria, Cilicia, and Asia (6:9). These were Hellenistic Jews who had deep investment in the temple as the center of Jewish identity. Stephen's claim that God transcends the temple threatened their theological framework. The stoning was technically illegal under Roman law (only Rome could execute), suggesting it was a mob action during a period of lax Roman oversight.

cross_refs.note: Heaven is my throne, earth my footstool — Stephen's climactic quote against temple-idolatry
Solomon's temple prayer: 'Heaven cannot contain you' — Stephen echoes the temple builder himself
'Stiff-necked people' — God's own term for Israel; Stephen applies it to the Sanhedrin
Son of Man at God's right hand — Stephen sees this vision fulfilled in real time
Jesus' cross prayers — Stephen echoes both: 'Receive my spirit' and 'Do not hold this sin against them'

acts_pentecost.divine_council_note: Stephen's vision is a divine council scene: 'I see the heavens opened, and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God' (7:56). Jesus is normally described as 'seated' at God's right hand (Ps 110:1). That he 'stands' is significant -- he rises as an advocate/judge. Some interpreters see Jesus standing to welcome Stephen into the council; others see him standing as a witness for Stephen's defense. Either way, the heavenly court is open and active, and the Son of Man presides.

sections.heading: God on the Move: Stephen's Portable Theology
sections.body: Stephen's speech is not a random history lesson but a carefully constructed argument: God has always been a God on the move. He appeared to Abraham in Mesopotamia (7:2) -- before the promised land. He was with Joseph in Egypt (7:9) -- outside the land entirely. He spoke to Moses at Sinai (7:30) -- in a desert, not a temple. The tabernacle was a TENT -- portable, mobile, going where God's people go. The temple, for all its glory, introduced a dangerous temptation: confining God to a building. Stephen quotes Isaiah 66:1: 'Heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool. What kind of house will you build for me?' The theological implication is explosive: if God is not confined to the temple, then salvation is not confined to Jerusalem. The gospel can go everywhere.

sections.heading: The Standing Son of Man: Heaven's Court Opens
sections.body: Stephen's vision (7:55-56) is the most dramatic divine council scene in Acts. He sees 'the heavens opened' (diēnoigmenous) -- the boundary between heaven and earth becomes transparent. He sees 'the Son of Man standing (hestōta) at the right hand of God.' Every other NT reference to Jesus' post-ascension position uses 'seated' (kathēmenon, from Ps 110:1). Why does he stand? Three possibilities, all rich: (1) He rises to welcome his first martyr home. (2) He stands as advocate, defending Stephen before the divine court. (3) He stands as judge, pronouncing verdict on those stoning his witness. All three may be simultaneously true. The Sanhedrin reacts with fury because Stephen claims to see what only the high priest was supposed to see: God's glory in the inner sanctum.

sections.heading: The Seed of the Church: Stephen's Death and Saul's Conversion
sections.body: Tertullian would later write: 'The blood of the martyrs is the seed of the church.' Stephen's death illustrates this principle. His prayer of forgiveness -- 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them' (7:60) -- echoes Jesus' cross prayer. And among those present, 'the witnesses laid down their garments at the feet of a young man named Saul' (7:58). Saul approved of Stephen's execution (8:1). Yet Stephen's prayer appears to have been answered in the most dramatic conversion in church history. The man who watched Stephen die would become the apostle to the nations. Augustine commented: 'If Stephen had not prayed, the church would not have had Paul.' The persecution that follows Stephen's death (8:1) scatters the church beyond Jerusalem -- fulfilling Jesus' commission (1:8) through the very mechanism the enemies intended as destruction.

acts_pentecost.title: Philip, Saul's Conversion, and the Cornelius Breakthrough
acts_pentecost.synopsis: The scattering from Stephen's persecution drives the gospel outward in concentric circles, exactly as Jesus predicted (1:8). Philip goes to Samaria -- the despised half-breed territory -- and 'proclaimed the Christ' (8:5). Samaritans believe and are baptized (8:12). Philip then encounters the Ethiopian eunuch on the Gaza road -- a God-fearer reading Isaiah 53 in his chariot (8:27-28). Philip explains: 'the good news about Jesus' (8:35). The eunuch is baptized and goes on his way rejoicing (8:39). Meanwhile, Saul breathes 'threats and murder' (9:1) against the church. On the Damascus road, a light brighter than the sun strikes him down: 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? I am Jesus' (9:4-5). Saul's conversion is narrated three times in Acts (chs 9, 22, 26) -- its importance cannot be overstated. The climactic breakthrough comes with Cornelius (ch 10): Peter receives a vision of unclean animals and the command 'What God has made clean, do not call common' (10:15). He goes to Cornelius' house, preaches, and the Holy Spirit falls on Gentiles BEFORE baptism (10:44). 'Can anyone withhold water?' Peter asks (10:47). The Jewish-Gentile barrier -- the wall of hostility (Eph 2:14) -- crumbles. The Jerusalem church is shocked but concedes: 'Then to the Gentiles also God has granted repentance that leads to life' (11:18).

key_verse.text: Truly I understand that God shows no partiality, but in every nation anyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him.

original_terms.meaning: Common/unclean — the purity label Peter must unlearn; God declares Gentiles clean
Eunuch — excluded from the assembly (Deut 23:1) but welcomed by Isaiah (56:3-5) and now by Philip
One who shows partiality/respects persons — Peter declares God is NOT this
Sojourner/God-fearer — Cornelius' status: worshipping Israel's God without full conversion
Unclean/impure — the entire clean/unclean system applied to people is overturned by God's word

acts_pentecost.ane_backdrop: The Jewish purity system distinguished clean from unclean in food, bodies, and peoples. Gentile homes were considered unclean -- entering one rendered a Jew ritually impure. Peter's vision overturns not just dietary law but the entire Jew-Gentile social boundary. The Ethiopian eunuch was doubly excluded: as a Gentile and as a eunuch (Deut 23:1). Yet Isaiah 56:3-5 promises eunuchs 'a name better than sons and daughters.' Philip's gospel fulfills Isaiah's inclusive vision.
acts_pentecost.second_temple: The 'God-fearers' (phoboumenoi ton theon) were Gentiles attracted to Judaism who worshipped Israel's God and attended synagogue but had not undergone full proselyte conversion (including circumcision). Cornelius was a Roman centurion in this category. His conversion without circumcision was revolutionary -- it challenged the Pharisaic position that Gentiles must become Jews to be saved.

cross_refs.note: The passage the Ethiopian eunuch was reading — the Suffering Servant led like a sheep to slaughter
Eunuchs promised a name and memorial in God's house — fulfilled in the eunuch's baptism
In Abraham all families blessed — the Cornelius episode begins fulfilling the Abrahamic promise
Christ broke down the dividing wall of hostility between Jew and Gentile
Nations allotted to other gods, Israel to YHWH — now the barrier is broken; all nations belong to God

acts_pentecost.divine_council_note: The Cornelius event is a Deuteronomy 32 reversal. The nations were allotted to lesser divine beings; Israel alone belonged to YHWH. Now God declares: 'in every nation anyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him' (10:35). The Spirit falls on Gentiles without requiring them to become Jews first (10:44-45). The gods of the nations are being displaced -- their peoples are being reclaimed by the Most High through the gospel of his Son.

sections.heading: Philip and the Ethiopian: Isaiah's Vision Fulfilled
sections.body: The Ethiopian eunuch is one of the most theologically loaded characters in Acts. As a eunuch, he was excluded from the assembly of Israel (Deut 23:1). As an Ethiopian, he represents the uttermost geographic reach (Acts 1:8 -- 'the end of the earth'). Yet he is reading Isaiah, the very prophet who promised: 'Let not the eunuch say, I am a dry tree... I will give in my house and within my walls a monument and a name better than sons and daughters' (Isa 56:3-5). He is reading Isaiah 53 -- the Suffering Servant. Philip explains that this servant is Jesus. The eunuch sees water and asks: 'What prevents me from being baptized?' (8:36). In the old system, everything prevented him. In the new, nothing does.

sections.heading: Damascus Road: The Persecutor Becomes the Apostle
sections.body: Saul's conversion is narrated three times in Acts (chs 9, 22, 26) because it is the most dramatic illustration of grace in the entire book. The persecutor of the church encounters the risen Lord and discovers that in persecuting Christians he was persecuting Christ himself: 'I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting' (9:5). The identification of Jesus with his people is total -- to touch them is to touch him. Saul is blinded for three days (mirroring Jonah's three days in the fish -- death and resurrection symbolism). Ananias of Damascus is told: 'He is a chosen instrument of mine to carry my name before the Gentiles and kings and the children of Israel' (9:15). The greatest enemy becomes the greatest missionary.

sections.heading: Cornelius: The Wall Falls
sections.body: The Cornelius episode (ch 10) is the turning point of Acts. Peter's vision of unclean animals (10:9-16) is not primarily about food -- it is about people. 'What God has made clean, do not call common' (10:15). When Peter arrives at Cornelius' house, he says: 'God has shown me that I should not call any person common or unclean' (10:28). As Peter preaches, the Holy Spirit falls on the Gentiles (10:44) -- BEFORE baptism, BEFORE any ritual, BEFORE any human decision. God acts first. The Jewish believers are 'amazed' (existēsan, 10:45) that the Spirit falls on Gentiles. Peter's question -- 'Can anyone withhold water for baptizing these people who have received the Holy Spirit just as we have?' (10:47) -- is rhetorical. God has spoken. The church must follow.

acts_journeys.title: The First Missionary Journey: Antioch to the Gentiles
acts_journeys.synopsis: The church at Antioch -- the first to include significant Gentile membership -- commissions Barnabas and Saul for mission (13:1-3). The Holy Spirit says: 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul for the work to which I have called them' (13:2). They sail to Cyprus (Barnabas' homeland), where Saul confronts the magician Bar-Jesus/Elymas and is first called 'Paul' (13:9). In Pisidian Antioch, Paul delivers his first major sermon (13:16-41), a masterful synthesis of Israel's history climaxing in Jesus' death and resurrection. He announces: 'Through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you, and by him everyone who believes is freed from everything from which you could not be freed by the law of Moses' (13:38-39). The Gentiles beg to hear more; the Jews grow jealous. Paul declares: 'Since you thrust it aside... behold, we are turning to the Gentiles. For so the Lord has commanded us: I have made you a light for the Gentiles' (13:46-47, quoting Isaiah 49:6). In Lystra, Paul heals a lame man and the crowd tries to worship Barnabas as Zeus and Paul as Hermes (14:11-12). Paul is stoned, left for dead, but rises and continues (14:19-20). They return to Antioch, reporting 'all that God had done with them, and how he had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles' (14:27).

key_verse.text: For so the Lord has commanded us, saying, 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth.'

original_terms.meaning: To set apart/separate — the Spirit's consecration of Paul and Barnabas for mission
Light to/of the Gentiles — from Isaiah 49:6; the Servant's mission now carried by the church
Light of nations (Hebrew) — Isaiah's phrase that defines the church's mission beyond Israel
To justify/declare righteous — Paul's first articulation of justification by faith (13:39)
Hermes — Greek messenger god; the Lystrans' cultural framework for understanding Paul's preaching

acts_journeys.ane_backdrop: Lystra's identification of Barnabas as Zeus and Paul as Hermes reflects local mythology. According to Ovid's Metamorphoses, Zeus and Hermes once visited Phrygia in human form and were rejected by all except an elderly couple, Philemon and Baucis. The Lystrans may have been determined not to make the same mistake. Paul's horror at the worship attempt shows the absolute divide between Christian theology and pagan accommodation.
acts_journeys.second_temple: Paul's Pisidian Antioch sermon follows the same pattern as Peter's Pentecost sermon and Stephen's speech: Israel's history retold with Jesus as the climax. But Paul adds a distinctive note: justification by faith apart from Torah observance (13:38-39). This becomes the theological earthquake that triggers the Jerusalem Council.

cross_refs.note: The Servant as 'a light for the Gentiles' — Paul applies the Servant's mission to the church
Quoted in Paul's sermon (13:33): 'You are my Son, today I have begotten you' — resurrection as enthronement
David's psalm applied to Jesus' resurrection (13:35) — the same argument Peter made at Pentecost
Paul's warning (13:41): 'Look, you scoffers, be astounded and perish' — judgment on unbelief
Paul later recalls his sufferings at Antioch, Iconium, and Lystra to Timothy

acts_journeys.divine_council_note: Paul's Pisidian Antioch sermon explicitly claims that Jesus' resurrection fulfills Psalm 2:7 ('You are my Son, today I have begotten you,' 13:33). In its original context, Psalm 2 is a divine council enthronement psalm where YHWH installs his king on Zion against the raging nations and their rulers. Paul applies the cosmic enthronement to the resurrection -- Jesus is installed as the divine king over all nations, which is why the gospel must go to the Gentiles.

sections.heading: Paul's Pisidian Antioch Sermon: Israel's Story Reaches Its Climax
sections.body: Paul's sermon in the synagogue (13:16-41) is a compressed history of salvation: God chose the patriarchs (13:17), delivered Israel from Egypt (13:17), gave them the land (13:19), appointed judges (13:20), gave them Saul then David as king (13:21-22). From David's offspring, 'God has brought to Israel a Savior, Jesus, as he promised' (13:23). The residents of Jerusalem and their rulers 'did not recognize him nor understand the utterances of the prophets' (13:27) -- the same theme as the Sower parable. They fulfilled prophecy by condemning him. But God raised him from the dead (13:30). The practical conclusion: 'through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you, and by him everyone who believes is freed from everything from which you could not be freed by the law of Moses' (13:38-39). This is Paul's first articulation of justification by faith.

sections.heading: Turning to the Gentiles: Isaiah's Mandate
sections.body: When the Jews of Pisidian Antioch reject the message (13:45), Paul makes a watershed declaration: 'Since you thrust it aside and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we are turning to the Gentiles' (13:46). He grounds this in Isaiah 49:6: 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth.' The Servant's mission in Isaiah was never exclusively about Israel -- it was always aimed at the nations. Paul sees the church inheriting and fulfilling this Servant vocation. The Gentiles respond with joy: 'as many as were appointed to eternal life believed' (13:48). The gospel pattern emerges: synagogue first, then Gentiles -- not because Jews are worthier but because the promise came to them first.

sections.heading: Stoned and Rising: The Pattern of Suffering and Advance
sections.body: In Lystra, Paul is stoned and 'dragged out of the city, supposing that he was dead' (14:19). But he rises, enters the city the next day, and continues to Derbe (14:20). This is the pattern of Acts: persecution does not stop the gospel but propels it. Paul later tells these same communities: 'Through many tribulations we must enter the kingdom of God' (14:22). Suffering is not an obstacle to the mission but part of it. The first journey ends with Paul and Barnabas reporting to Antioch that 'God had opened a door of faith to the Gentiles' (14:27). The metaphor is significant -- God opens doors that human gatekeepers cannot close.

acts_journeys.title: The Jerusalem Council: Must Gentiles Become Jews?
acts_journeys.synopsis: Acts 15 records the most consequential church meeting in history. 'Some men came down from Judea and were teaching the brothers: Unless you are circumcised according to the custom of Moses, you cannot be saved' (15:1). This triggers 'no small dissension' (15:2). Paul and Barnabas go to Jerusalem to settle the matter. Peter testifies about Cornelius: God 'made no distinction between us and them, having cleansed their hearts by faith' (15:9). He asks: 'Why are you putting God to the test by placing a yoke on the neck of the disciples that neither our fathers nor we have been able to bear?' (15:10). Paul and Barnabas report Gentile conversions. Then James speaks -- the Lord's brother, the leader of the Jerusalem church. He quotes Amos 9:11-12 (LXX): 'After this I will return and rebuild the tent of David... that the remnant of mankind may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles who are called by my name' (15:16-17). James' interpretation: God is rebuilding David's fallen dynasty through Jesus precisely SO THAT Gentiles can be included as Gentiles, without becoming Jews. The council's decree: Gentiles need not be circumcised but should abstain from things polluted by idols, sexual immorality, things strangled, and blood (15:20) -- minimal requirements for Jewish-Gentile table fellowship, not a new Torah.

key_verse.text: But we believe that we will be saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as they will.

original_terms.meaning: Circumcision — the covenant sign of Gen 17; the question: is it required for Gentile salvation?
Booth/tent of David — Amos' image of David's fallen dynasty; rebuilt in Jesus to include the nations
Yoke — Peter describes Torah observance as an unbearable yoke for Gentiles
Grace — the principle that settles the debate: salvation by grace, not by Torah compliance
Resident alien — the OT status for righteous Gentiles; the Council's requirements echo Noahide/ger laws

acts_journeys.ane_backdrop: In the Greco-Roman world, religious identity was tied to ethnic identity. Jews were recognized as an ethnos with their own customs (circumcision, sabbath, dietary laws). The question at the council was whether Christianity would remain a Jewish sect requiring ethnic markers or become a universal movement accessible to all peoples. The four prohibitions (15:20) correspond roughly to the 'Noahide laws' -- universal moral requirements for righteous Gentiles in Jewish tradition.
acts_journeys.second_temple: The Qumran community required full conversion to their interpretation of Torah for membership. Pharisees debated the requirements for proselytes (circumcision, mikveh, sacrifice). The school of Shammai demanded stricter observance; Hillel was more lenient. James' ruling aligns with neither school but introduces a new category: Gentiles saved as Gentiles, with minimal requirements for communal harmony.

cross_refs.note: James' proof text: God rebuilds David's tent so that Gentiles called by God's name may seek him
The circumcision covenant — the issue at stake: is this required for all God's people?
Paul's account of the same council meeting — adds details about private meetings
Source of the four prohibitions: blood, strangled animals, sexual immorality, idolatry
The nations streaming to Zion — the eschatological vision the Council begins fulfilling

acts_journeys.divine_council_note: James quotes Amos 9:11-12 in the LXX form: God will 'rebuild the tent (skēnē) of David... so that the remnant of mankind may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles who are called by my name.' This is a Deuteronomy 32 reversal: the nations formerly under lesser gods are now 'called by YHWH's name' -- they belong to him directly. David's rebuilt 'tent' is not a physical building but the Messianic kingdom, which extends divine council membership to the Gentiles.

sections.heading: The Crisis: Circumcision as Salvation Requirement
sections.body: The Judaizers' position was not unreasonable from a traditional standpoint: God commanded circumcision in Genesis 17 as an 'everlasting covenant' (berit olam). Abraham's descendants and any alien joining the community must be circumcised. The question was: does the Messiah's coming change this? Paul and Barnabas insisted it does -- the Spirit was given to uncircumcised Gentiles (the Cornelius precedent). Peter, who witnessed Cornelius' conversion firsthand, agrees: 'God made no distinction between us and them, having cleansed their hearts by faith' (15:9). His statement in 15:11 is remarkable for its direction: 'We (Jews) believe that we will be saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as THEY (Gentiles) will' -- not Gentiles saved like Jews, but Jews saved like Gentiles: by grace.

sections.heading: James and Amos: The Rebuilt Tent of David
sections.body: James' use of Amos 9:11-12 is the hermeneutical key to the entire council. Amos prophesied that God would rebuild David's fallen 'booth' (sukkah) -- the dynasty destroyed in 586 BC. James sees this fulfilled in Jesus' resurrection and enthronement. But the PURPOSE of rebuilding is stated: 'so that the remnant of mankind may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles who are called by my name.' The Davidic restoration exists FOR Gentile inclusion. If Gentiles must become Jews to be saved, Amos' prophecy fails -- they would not be 'Gentiles called by God's name' but converts called by Moses' name. James' logic is that Gentiles must remain Gentiles for prophecy to be fulfilled.

sections.heading: The Four Prohibitions: Minimum for Unity, Not a Mini-Torah
sections.body: The council's decree (15:20,29) requires Gentile believers to abstain from: (1) things polluted by idols (idol meat), (2) sexual immorality (porneia), (3) things strangled, and (4) blood. These are not a shortened Torah but minimum requirements for Jewish-Gentile table fellowship. All four correspond to commands given to resident aliens (gerim) in Leviticus 17-18 -- the rules that applied to non-Israelites living among Israel. The practical concern was fellowship: Jewish and Gentile believers needed to eat together, and these minimal restrictions enabled shared meals. The decree preserved both Gentile freedom from Torah and Jewish sensitivity about purity -- a pastoral masterpiece.

acts_journeys.title: Into Europe: Philippi, Athens, and Corinth
acts_journeys.synopsis: Paul's second journey takes the gospel into Europe for the first time. In Troas, a vision calls him to Macedonia: 'Come over and help us' (16:9). In Philippi, the first European convert is Lydia, a dealer in purple cloth and a 'worshiper of God' (sebomenē ton theon, 16:14). Paul and Silas are imprisoned after exorcising a slave girl's spirit; at midnight they sing hymns, an earthquake opens the prison, and the Philippian jailer believes: 'What must I do to be saved?' -- 'Believe in the Lord Jesus' (16:30-31). In Thessalonica, Paul argues from Scripture that 'it was necessary for the Christ to suffer and to rise' (17:3). In Athens, Paul encounters the intellectual capital of the ancient world. At the Areopagus, he delivers his most culturally adapted sermon (17:22-31): beginning from their altar 'To the Unknown God' (Agnōstō Theō), he proclaims the Creator who 'made from one man every nation of mankind' (17:26), quotes the Greek poets Epimenides and Aratus ('In him we live and move and have our being... we are his offspring,' 17:28), and announces the resurrection as proof of coming judgment. Some mock, others believe. In Corinth, Paul spends 18 months establishing the church, supported by tentmakers Aquila and Priscilla. The Lord tells him in a vision: 'Do not be afraid... I have many in this city who are my people' (18:9-10).

key_verse.text: And he made from one man every nation of mankind to live on all the face of the earth, having determined allotted periods and the boundaries of their dwelling place, that they should seek God, and perhaps feel their way toward him and find him.

original_terms.meaning: Areopagus/Mars Hill — Athens' intellectual forum where Paul engages Greek philosophy with Hebrew theology
To an unknown god — the Athenian altar Paul uses as a starting point for gospel proclamation
Determined boundaries — echoes Deut 32:8 where God set the boundaries of peoples according to the sons of God
Repentance/mind-change — God commands 'all people everywhere to repent' (17:30)
Resurrection — the Athens audience thought Paul was preaching a new goddess named 'Anastasis'

acts_journeys.ane_backdrop: Athens in the first century was no longer a political power but remained the intellectual capital of the Mediterranean. Epicureans (matter is all, gods are distant) and Stoics (god pervades everything, live according to nature) debated in the agora daily. Paul's Areopagus speech engages both schools: against Epicureans, God is near and active; against Stoics, God is personal and will judge. His quotation of their own poets shows remarkable cultural literacy.
acts_journeys.second_temple: Paul's statement that God 'made from one man every nation' (17:26) and 'determined allotted periods and the boundaries of their dwelling place' directly alludes to the Table of Nations (Gen 10) and the Deuteronomy 32:8 tradition of nations allotted to divine beings. Paul is telling the Athenians: your nation was under lesser gods, but the true Creator now calls you directly. The 'times of ignorance' (17:30) God overlooked are ending -- the nations are being recalled from their assigned gods.

cross_refs.note: God set the boundaries of peoples — Paul alludes to this in 17:26, claiming God determined national boundaries
God formed the earth to be inhabited and calls all nations to himself — the vision behind Paul's Athens sermon
The Table of Nations — the original division of peoples that Paul references in 17:26
Paul's later letter to the Philippian church founded in Acts 16
Paul's summary of the Thessalonians' conversion — 'you turned from idols to serve the living God'

acts_journeys.divine_council_note: Paul's Areopagus speech is a divine council reclamation sermon addressed to Gentiles. He tells the Athenians that God 'determined allotted periods and the boundaries of their dwelling place' (17:26) -- directly alluding to the Deuteronomy 32:8 division of nations among divine beings. But the purpose was 'that they should seek God' (17:27). The 'times of ignorance' (17:30) when nations worshipped their assigned gods are now over. God 'commands all people everywhere to repent' (17:30) because he has appointed a man (Jesus) to judge the world, proven by raising him from the dead.

sections.heading: Philippi: The Gospel Crosses into Europe
sections.body: The Macedonian call (16:9) is a pivot point in world history -- the gospel enters Europe. Philippi was a Roman colony, proud of its Roman identity. The first convert, Lydia, is a businesswoman from Thyatira dealing in expensive purple cloth. Her conversion as 'the Lord opened her heart' (16:14) echoes the Sower parable -- good soil responding to the word. The Philippian jailer represents the opposite end of society: a Roman government employee, possibly a veteran. His desperate question -- 'What must I do to be saved?' (16:30) -- receives the simplest answer in Acts: 'Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved, you and your household' (16:31). Purple merchant and prison guard, rich and poor, woman and man -- the gospel gathers all.

sections.heading: Athens: The Unknown God Made Known
sections.body: Paul's Areopagus sermon is the most sophisticated cross-cultural gospel presentation in the New Testament. He begins not with Scripture (which his audience doesn't know) but with their own religious instinct: the altar 'To the Unknown God' (17:23). He identifies this unknown God as the Creator 'who made the world and everything in it' (17:24). He quotes their own poets: Epimenides ('in him we live and move and have our being') and Aratus ('we are his offspring,' 17:28). But he drives toward the specifically Christian claim: God 'has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed; and of this he has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead' (17:31). The resurrection divides the audience: some mock, some believe. Paul does not water down the scandal of bodily resurrection to appease Greek sensibilities.

sections.heading: Corinth: Fear, Vision, and 18 Months of Gospel Work
sections.body: Paul arrives in Corinth 'in weakness and in fear and much trembling' (1 Cor 2:3) -- Athens' cool reception may have shaken him. Corinth was notoriously immoral, commercially driven, and socially stratified. Yet the Lord speaks in a night vision: 'Do not be afraid, but go on speaking and do not be silent, for I am with you, and no one will attack you to harm you, for I have many in this city who are my people' (18:9-10). The promise 'I have many in this city' means God's elect are already there, unknown even to themselves, waiting for the gospel to call them forth. Paul stays 18 months -- his longest single-city ministry in Acts. The church he plants will receive two of his most important letters.

acts_journeys.title: Ephesus, the Riot, and Paul's Farewell to the Elders
acts_journeys.synopsis: Paul's third journey centers on Ephesus, where he spends over two years (19:10). He finds disciples who know only John's baptism and imparts the Holy Spirit (19:1-7). 'God was doing extraordinary miracles by the hands of Paul' (19:11) -- even handkerchiefs touched by Paul healed the sick. The seven sons of Sceva attempt unauthorized exorcism: 'I adjure you by the Jesus whom Paul proclaims' (19:13). The evil spirit replies: 'Jesus I know, and Paul I recognize, but who are you?' (19:15) and attacks them. Believers burn their magic books -- 50,000 pieces of silver worth (19:19). The silversmiths who profit from Artemis worship start a riot, chanting 'Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!' (19:28) for two hours. The city clerk calms the crowd. Paul departs for Macedonia and Greece, then returns along the coast. At Miletus, he delivers his farewell address to the Ephesian elders (20:17-38) -- the most personal speech in Acts: 'I did not shrink from declaring to you the whole counsel (boulē) of God' (20:27). He warns of 'fierce wolves' who will 'arise from among your own selves' (20:29-30). 'And now I commend you to God and to the word of his grace' (20:32). They weep, embrace, and grieve that 'they would not see his face again' (20:38).

key_verse.text: For I did not shrink from declaring to you the whole counsel of God.

original_terms.meaning: Counsel/plan/purpose — the comprehensive divine plan that Paul declared in its entirety
Artemis/Diana — the great goddess of Ephesus; the gospel threatens her economic cult
Overseer/bishop — the role Paul assigns to the Ephesian elders (20:28)
Wolf — false teachers who infiltrate the flock (20:29); echoes Jesus' warning in Matt 7:15
Counsel/plan (Hebrew) — God's sovereign purpose that Paul faithfully proclaimed

acts_journeys.ane_backdrop: The Temple of Artemis at Ephesus was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. Artemis (Roman Diana) was worshipped as a fertility goddess; her temple served as a bank and asylum. Silver shrines of Artemis were a major industry -- Demetrius the silversmith's complaint (19:24-27) was economic as much as religious. The gospel threatened both the spiritual and financial foundations of Ephesian society.
acts_journeys.second_temple: The sons of Sceva attempted to use Jesus' name as a magical formula (19:13), reflecting a common practice in Jewish and Greco-Roman magical traditions. The Greek Magical Papyri frequently invoke the names of Jewish patriarchs and angels alongside pagan deities. The demon's response ('Jesus I know, and Paul I recognize, but who are you?') demonstrates that spiritual authority flows from relationship, not incantation.

cross_refs.note: Paul's later letter to Ephesus: the battle is against 'cosmic powers over this present darkness'
Paul later sends Timothy to Ephesus to deal with the very false teachers he predicted in 20:29-30
Jesus' warning about wolves in sheep's clothing — Paul applies the same image to the church
The risen Christ's message to the Ephesian church — commending their orthodoxy but warning they've lost first love
God's 7,000 who have not bowed to Baal — Paul's confidence that God has his people everywhere

acts_journeys.divine_council_note: Paul's farewell declares 'the whole counsel (boulē) of God' (20:27). The word boulē means the deliberated purpose of a council -- in this case, the divine council's comprehensive plan for redemption. Paul has not held back any part of God's cosmic plan: creation, fall, election of Israel, the incarnation, cross, resurrection, Spirit, and the coming judgment. The 'fierce wolves' who will arise (20:29) are ultimately instruments of the adversary seeking to derail the council's plan.

sections.heading: Ephesus: Power Encounter with the Occult
sections.body: Ephesus was famous throughout the ancient world for magic. The 'Ephesian letters' (Ephesia grammata) were six magical words believed to carry power. Paul's ministry there is a direct power encounter: genuine miracles through Paul vs. counterfeit magic in the city. The sons of Sceva incident (19:13-16) dramatically demonstrates the difference. The demon recognizes Jesus and Paul but attacks unauthorized exorcists. The result: 'fear fell upon them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was extolled' (19:17). Believers bring their magic books and burn them publicly -- 50,000 pieces of silver, roughly 150 years' wages. This is not just personal repentance but public, economic renunciation of an entire spiritual system. 'So the word of the Lord continued to increase and prevail mightily' (19:20).

sections.heading: Great Is Artemis: When the Gospel Threatens Economies
sections.body: Demetrius the silversmith frames his complaint in religious terms ('this Paul has persuaded a great many people that gods made with hands are not gods,' 19:26), but his real concern is economic: 'there is danger not only that this trade of ours may come into disrepute' (19:27). The gospel always threatens economies built on idolatry. Two hours of chanting 'Great is Artemis!' cannot drown out a truth that is quietly converting the city's customers. The city clerk's cool-headed intervention (19:35-41) demonstrates that the gospel is not lawless -- it advances through persuasion, not violence.

sections.heading: Paul's Farewell: The Whole Counsel of God
sections.body: Paul's Miletus address (20:17-38) is his only speech to a Christian audience in Acts. It reveals his pastoral heart and his awareness that his mission is nearing its end. He describes his ministry: 'I did not shrink from declaring anything profitable... testifying both to Jews and to Greeks of repentance toward God and of faith in our Lord Jesus Christ' (20:20-21). He is 'constrained by the Spirit' to go to Jerusalem, knowing imprisonment and afflictions await (20:22-23). His charge to the elders: 'Pay careful attention to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers, to care for the church of God, which he obtained with his own blood' (20:28). The phrase 'his own blood' refers to Jesus -- the church's price tag is the blood of God's own Son. This is one of the highest Christological statements in Acts.

acts_trial.title: Arrest in Jerusalem: Paul Before the Mob and the Sanhedrin
acts_trial.synopsis: Despite prophetic warnings (21:4,10-11 -- Agabus binds his own hands and feet with Paul's belt, prophesying his arrest), Paul insists on going to Jerusalem: 'I am ready not only to be imprisoned but even to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus' (21:13). At the temple, Asian Jews accuse him of bringing Gentiles into the inner courts (21:28) -- a capital offense. The mob drags him out and begins beating him. Roman soldiers rescue him. From the barracks steps, Paul addresses the crowd in Aramaic (Hebraidi dialektō, 22:2), telling his conversion story: the Damascus road vision, Ananias' words, his commission to the Gentiles. The crowd listens until the word 'Gentiles' (ethnē, 22:21) -- then they erupt: 'Away with such a fellow from the earth!' (22:22). The Roman tribune prepares to flog Paul for information, but Paul reveals his Roman citizenship (22:25-28). Before the Sanhedrin, Paul exploits the Pharisee-Sadducee divide: 'I am a Pharisee... I am on trial concerning the hope of the resurrection of the dead' (23:6). The council erupts into chaos. That night, 'the Lord stood by him and said: Take courage, for as you have testified to the facts about me in Jerusalem, so you must testify also in Rome' (23:11) -- the divine mandate that drives the rest of Acts.

key_verse.text: The following night the Lord stood by him and said, 'Take courage, for as you have testified to the facts about me in Jerusalem, so you must testify also in Rome.'

original_terms.meaning: In the Hebrew/Aramaic language — Paul switches from Greek to Aramaic to connect with the Jerusalem crowd
Roman citizen — Paul's legal status that protects him from arbitrary flogging and grants appeal to Caesar
Resurrection of the dead — the core doctrine dividing Pharisees and Sadducees; Paul's strategic emphasis
Take courage/be of good cheer — the Lord's direct encouragement to Paul in prison
Ban/oath of destruction — 40+ men take an oath not to eat until they kill Paul (23:12); devotion-to-destruction language

acts_trial.ane_backdrop: The Jerusalem temple had stone barriers (the soreg) separating the Court of the Gentiles from the inner courts. Inscriptions in Greek and Latin warned: 'No foreigner may enter within the barricade around the sanctuary. Anyone caught doing so will bear the responsibility for his own ensuing death.' Two such inscriptions have been found archaeologically. The accusation against Paul -- bringing Greeks past this barrier -- was a capital charge the Romans permitted Jews to enforce.
acts_trial.second_temple: Paul's strategy before the Sanhedrin (23:6-9) exploits a genuine theological divide. The Sadducees denied resurrection, angels, and spirits (23:8); the Pharisees affirmed all three. By framing his case as a resurrection question, Paul effectively splits the council. Some Pharisees defend him: 'What if a spirit or an angel spoke to him?' (23:9). This shows that Paul's Damascus road experience was interpretable within Pharisaic categories -- divine revelation through angelophany.

cross_refs.note: Paul's original commission: 'He must suffer for the sake of my name' — now being fulfilled
Jesus predicted disciples would stand before councils and governors — Paul fulfills this
Christ broke down the 'dividing wall of hostility' — the temple barrier Paul is accused of violating
Daniel in the den of lions — faithful witness in the face of political conspiracy, like Paul's 40 assassins
Paul's later reflection: imprisonment has 'served to advance the gospel'

acts_trial.divine_council_note: The Lord's night vision to Paul (23:11) is a direct divine council commissioning: 'You MUST (dei -- divine necessity) testify also in Rome.' Paul's journey to Rome is not a human legal proceeding but a divinely ordained mission. The conspirators' oath to kill Paul (23:12-15) is a human attempt to thwart the divine council's plan -- it fails through Paul's nephew's intelligence (23:16-22). Human plots cannot override heaven's agenda.

sections.heading: The Trigger Word: 'Gentiles'
sections.body: Paul's defense speech on the temple steps (22:1-21) is a masterful retelling of his conversion. The crowd listens quietly through the Damascus road vision, through Ananias' words, through Paul's return to Jerusalem. But the moment he quotes Jesus' commission -- 'Go, for I will send you far away to the Gentiles (ethnē)' (22:21) -- they explode. The word 'Gentiles' is the trigger. They can tolerate a Jewish prophet's mystical experience; they cannot tolerate the inclusion of Gentiles in God's salvation plan without Torah compliance. The temple's dividing wall is not just architectural; it is theological. Paul's message that God has torn down that wall is what the crowd finds unbearable.

sections.heading: Roman Citizenship: Providence Through Politics
sections.body: Paul's Roman citizenship (22:25-28) is not a random biographical detail but a providential tool. Citizenship protected Paul from flogging without trial, gave him the right to a fair hearing before Roman courts, and ultimately enabled his appeal to Caesar (25:11), which transported him to Rome. The tribune paid 'a large sum' for his citizenship (22:28); Paul 'was born a citizen' (22:28) -- suggesting his family had served Rome in some capacity. God uses political structures for gospel purposes. The same empire that crucified Jesus will, through its own legal system, transport his apostle to its capital.

sections.heading: The Lord's Night Visit: Divine Mandate for Rome
sections.body: In the darkness of the Roman barracks, 'the Lord stood by him' (23:11). The verb paristēmi ('stood by') suggests physical presence -- a theophany in a prison cell. The message has three parts: (1) 'Take courage' (tharsei) -- the same word Jesus spoke to the disciples on the storm-tossed sea (Matt 14:27). (2) 'As you have testified in Jerusalem' -- Paul's Jerusalem witness is accepted and sufficient. (3) 'You MUST testify also in Rome' -- dei, the word of divine necessity. This dei drives the entire final quarter of Acts. Every obstacle between Jerusalem and Rome -- assassins, governors, storms, shipwreck -- is merely a delay, not a cancellation, of God's decreed plan.

acts_trial.title: Before Governors and Kings: Felix, Festus, and Agrippa
acts_trial.synopsis: Paul is transferred to Caesarea under heavy guard (23:23 -- 470 soldiers for one prisoner) after the plot of 40 assassins is discovered. Before Governor Felix, the high priest's lawyer Tertullus accuses Paul of being 'a plague, one who stirs up riots... a ringleader of the sect of the Nazarenes' (24:5). Paul defends himself: 'According to the Way, which they call a sect, I worship the God of our fathers, believing everything laid down by the Law and written in the Prophets, having a hope in God that there will be a resurrection of both the just and the unjust' (24:14-15). Felix, who has 'a rather accurate knowledge of the Way' (24:22), delays judgment. Paul speaks to Felix and his wife Drusilla 'about righteousness, self-control, and the coming judgment' -- Felix becomes 'alarmed' (emphobos genomenos, 24:25) and dismisses Paul, hoping for a bribe (24:26). Two years pass. Festus replaces Felix and reopens the case. Paul appeals to Caesar (25:11) -- his legal right as a Roman citizen. Before King Agrippa II and Bernice, Paul delivers his most complete defense (26:1-29), including his third account of the Damascus road. He tells Agrippa: 'I stand here testifying both to small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would come to pass: that the Christ must suffer and that, by being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles' (26:22-23). Agrippa responds: 'In a short time would you persuade me to be a Christian?' Paul answers: 'Whether short or long, I would to God that not only you but also all who hear me this day might become such as I am -- except for these chains' (26:29). Agrippa concludes: 'This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar' (26:32).

key_verse.text: To this day I have had the help that comes from God, and so I stand here testifying both to small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would come to pass: that the Christ must suffer and that, by being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles.

original_terms.meaning: The Way — the earliest name for Christianity; a movement, not just a belief system
Sect/faction — what Paul's accusers call 'the Way'; Paul rejects the label by affirming continuity with Moses and the prophets
Christian — Agrippa uses the term; only appears three times in the NT (here, 11:26, 1 Pet 4:16)
Goads — 'It is hard for you to kick against the goads' (26:14); a Greek proverb about resisting divine will
To open (eyes) — Paul's commission: 'to open their eyes' (26:18), the Servant's mission from Isaiah 42:7

acts_trial.ane_backdrop: Felix was a freedman who rose to procurator of Judea (AD 52-59). Tacitus says he 'exercised the power of a king with the spirit of a slave' (Histories 5.9). His wife Drusilla was a Jewish princess, sister of Agrippa II. Agrippa II was the last Herodian king, ruling territories northeast of Galilee. The political context was volatile: Jewish nationalism was intensifying toward the revolt of AD 66.
acts_trial.second_temple: Paul consistently frames his defense as continuity with Judaism, not departure from it: 'I worship the God of our fathers, believing everything laid down by the Law and written in the Prophets' (24:14). His claim is that the resurrection of Jesus is the fulfillment of the hope of Israel -- not a novel religion but Israel's faith brought to completion. The Pharisaic hope in resurrection (24:15) is confirmed by Jesus' being 'the first to rise from the dead' (26:23).

cross_refs.note: The Servant will 'open the eyes that are blind' — Paul's commission (26:18) fulfills the Servant's mission
'A light for the Gentiles' — Paul again applies this to his ministry (26:23)
Jesus predicted his followers would stand before kings and governors — Paul fulfills this before Felix, Festus, Agrippa
Paul later writes: 'The Lord stood by me and strengthened me, so that the message might be fully proclaimed'
Kings and rulers rage against the Lord's anointed — the pattern repeated in Paul's trials

acts_trial.divine_council_note: Paul's commission as stated to Agrippa (26:17-18) is explicitly cosmic: Jesus sends him 'to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power (exousia) of Satan to God.' The mission is not merely educational but a transfer of spiritual authority -- people moving from Satan's domain to God's. This is Deuteronomy 32 reversal language: the nations under rebellious spiritual powers are being reclaimed for the Most High.

sections.heading: Felix Alarmed: Righteousness, Self-Control, and Judgment
sections.body: Paul's private conversation with Felix and Drusilla (24:24-25) reveals his pastoral courage. He speaks of 'righteousness, self-control, and the coming judgment.' Felix was notoriously corrupt; Drusilla had been persuaded to leave her first husband. Paul does not flatter his powerful audience; he speaks truth to power. Felix becomes 'alarmed' (emphobos -- literally 'in fear') and dismisses Paul with 'When I get an opportunity I will summon you.' The opportunity never comes. Felix keeps Paul in prison for two years, hoping for a bribe (24:26). The text is devastating in its restraint: the Roman governor knows the truth, fears the judgment, and still refuses to repent because he prefers money to salvation.

sections.heading: Paul Before Agrippa: The Gospel in Three Minutes
sections.body: Paul's defense before Agrippa (26:1-29) is his most polished presentation. He retells the Damascus road (for the third time in Acts) but adds details: the voice spoke in Hebrew/Aramaic (26:14), saying 'It is hard for you to kick against the goads' -- a Greek proverb meaning 'you cannot resist the divine will.' His commission is stated with maximum clarity: 'to open their eyes, so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me' (26:18). The gospel in one sentence. Paul's final appeal to Agrippa is deeply personal: 'King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe' (26:27). Agrippa deflects with his famous reply. Paul's response ('except for these chains') combines wit, humility, and genuine longing for Agrippa's conversion.

sections.heading: 'Could Have Been Set Free': Providence Over Liberty
sections.body: Agrippa's verdict is bittersweet: 'This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar' (26:32). From a human perspective, Paul made a legal miscalculation. From the divine perspective, the appeal to Caesar is the vehicle that takes the gospel to Rome. The Lord had said: 'You MUST (dei) testify in Rome' (23:11). Paul's chains are not a failure of justice but a means of divine transportation. As Paul himself later writes from Rome: 'What has happened to me has really served to advance the gospel, so that it has become known throughout the whole imperial guard' (Phil 1:12-13). Every guard chained to Paul for a four-hour shift became a captive audience for the gospel.

acts_trial.title: Shipwreck, Malta, and the Gospel Reaches Rome
acts_trial.synopsis: The sea voyage to Rome (ch 27) is the most detailed account of ancient Mediterranean navigation in all of literature. Paul, as a prisoner, warns the centurion Julius against sailing past Fair Havens on Crete: 'I perceive that the voyage will be with injury and much loss' (27:10). Ignored, the ship is caught in a northeaster (Euraquilo, 27:14). For fourteen days, 276 people are battered by storm. All hope of salvation is abandoned (27:20). Then Paul stands: 'There stood by me this night an angel of the God to whom I belong and whom I worship, and he said, Do not be afraid, Paul; you must (dei) stand before Caesar. And behold, God has granted you all those who sail with you' (27:23-24). They shipwreck on Malta. Paul is bitten by a viper and suffers no harm (28:3-5) -- the natives first think he is a murderer being judged, then change their minds and call him 'a god' (28:6). Paul heals the father of Publius (Malta's chief man) and others. After three months, they sail to Rome. Paul arrives and meets Roman believers who come to greet him. In Rome, he spends two years under house arrest (28:30), 'proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness (parrēsia) and without hindrance (akōlytōs)' (28:31). Acts ends not with a period but with a conjunction -- the story is ongoing.

key_verse.text: He lived there two whole years at his own expense, and welcomed all who came to him, proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness and without hindrance.

original_terms.meaning: Angel of God — the divine messenger who assures Paul of safe arrival (27:23)
It is necessary/must — divine necessity; 'you MUST stand before Caesar' (27:24) — the same dei from 23:11
Without hindrance/unhindered — Acts' final word, describing the gospel's unstoppable advance
Boldness/free speech — paired with 'unhindered'; the gospel proclaims itself freely even from a prison
Kingdom of God — the subject of Paul's preaching; the same theme from Jesus' first proclamation in Mark 1:15

acts_trial.ane_backdrop: Ancient Mediterranean navigation was seasonal -- the mare clausum (closed sea) from November to March made winter sailing extremely dangerous. The ship's route -- Fair Havens, Phoenix, Malta -- is archaeologically and geographically confirmed. Luke's nautical detail (wind directions, anchoring techniques, jettisoning cargo, running aground) is praised by maritime historians as the most accurate ancient account of a sea voyage.
acts_trial.second_temple: Paul's final scene in Rome (28:23-28) parallels his pattern throughout Acts: he invites Jewish leaders, expounds the kingdom from Moses and the Prophets, and receives a divided response. He quotes Isaiah 6:9-10 (the hardening text Jesus used in Matt 13:14-15): 'This people's heart has grown dull.' He concludes: 'Let it be known to you that this salvation of God has been sent to the Gentiles; they will listen' (28:28). The pattern from Acts 13:46 and 18:6 reaches its climax.

cross_refs.note: Paul's final Scripture citation (28:26-27) — the same hardening text Jesus quoted for the parables
Storm at sea, prophet in danger, pagan sailors — Paul's voyage echoes Jonah's but inverts the outcome
They will pick up serpents and not be harmed — fulfilled in Paul's viper encounter on Malta
Paul's Roman imprisonment 'served to advance the gospel' — written during Acts 28's period
Paul's earlier letter: 'I am eager to preach the gospel to you also who are in Rome' — now fulfilled

acts_trial.divine_council_note: The angel's message (27:23-24) reveals heaven's governance over earthly events: 'God has granted (kecharistai) you all those who sail with you.' The 276 people on the ship are saved because of God's purpose for Paul. The word kecharistai is from charizomai ('to grant as a grace gift') -- their lives are a gift of grace connected to Paul's mission. Acts' final word, akōlytōs ('unhindered'), declares that no human power -- not the Sanhedrin, not Roman governors, not storms, not vipers -- can hinder what the divine council has decreed.

sections.heading: The Storm: 276 People Saved for Paul's Sake
sections.body: The shipwreck narrative (ch 27) is not a digression from Luke's theological purposes but its culmination. The storm symbolizes the opposition the gospel faces on its way to Rome -- and the divine guarantee that it will arrive. Paul's calm leadership amid panic demonstrates the principle: God's servants are safest in the most dangerous places when God has decreed their mission. The angel's promise -- 'God has granted you all those who sail with you' (27:24) -- means the entire ship's company is preserved because of one man's divine appointment. This echoes the pattern of Sodom: the righteous preserve the community (Gen 18:23-32). Paul's instruction to eat (27:33-36) becomes a eucharistic moment: he takes bread, gives thanks, breaks it, and distributes it to 276 souls.

sections.heading: Malta: Viper, Healing, and the Gospel's Reach
sections.body: The Malta episode (28:1-10) bookends with the Lystra incident from the first journey. In Lystra, the crowd called Paul a god after a healing; on Malta, they call him a god after the viper incident (28:6). The snake attaching to Paul's hand and causing no harm echoes Mark 16:18 and demonstrates that the power of the curse (Gen 3:14-15) is broken for those carrying the gospel. Paul heals Publius' father of fever and dysentery (28:8) and then the entire island's sick (28:9). Malta becomes an unplanned mission field -- further evidence that God's plan is bigger than human itineraries.

sections.heading: Rome at Last: Unhindered and Unstoppable
sections.body: Acts' ending is deliberately open. Paul arrives in Rome, meets with Jewish leaders, preaches the kingdom, and receives a divided response (28:23-24). He quotes Isaiah 6:9-10 -- the hardening text -- and declares salvation sent to the Gentiles. Then Luke records: 'He lived there two whole years... proclaiming the kingdom of God and teaching about the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness and without hindrance' (28:30-31). Why doesn't Luke record Paul's trial outcome? Because the point of Acts is not Paul's fate but the gospel's unstoppable advance. The final word -- akōlytōs ('without hindrance') -- is programmatic: chains cannot stop the word. Rome, the seat of world power, has become the latest mission field. The gospel that began in a Jerusalem upper room now sounds in the capital of the empire. Acts 1:8 is fulfilled: Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, the end of the earth.
